raw_case:
Prenatal ultrasound assisted diagnosis of de-novo terminal 7q deletion syndrome: A case report with literature review
Terminal 7q deletion is a rare chromosomal anomaly resulting from partial deletion of the long arm of chromosome 7. 7q terminal deletion syndrome results in variable clinical phenotypes, such as microcephaly, holoprosencephaly, craniofacial abnormalities, sacral hypoplasia, etc. We report a case of prenatal diagnosis of this syndrome with multiple abnormalities including holoprosencephaly and other craniofacial anomalies seen on ultrasound seen at NT scan. Pregnancy was terminated and chromosomal microarray showed ∼5.5 MB deletion in chromosome 7 spanning the 7q36.2-36.3 region. In addition, a literature review was done to enlist the various prenatal ultrasound features of this rare syndrome.

Introduction

Terminal 7q deletion or distal 7q monosomy is a rare chromosomal anomaly worldwide resulting from partial deletion of the long arm of chromosome 7 [1]. Due to the small number of cases documented in the literature, the precise incidence of the illness is unknown. Holoprosencephaly (HPE), microcephaly, intrauterine growth retardation, sacral dysgenesis, cranial-facial abnormalities, and other traits are among the key hallmarks of the widely diverse phenotype of the condition [2].We present a case of several prenatal ultrasonography abnormalities seen during the NT scan. After the pregnancy terminated, the abortus underwent chromosomal microarray (CMA), which revealed a ∼5.5 MB deletion in the 7q36.2-36.3 region of chromosome 7. We also reviewed the literature to compare the different prenatal ultrasonography characteristics of this uncommon disease.To the best of our knowledge, this is the first case of terminal 7q deletion syndrome from the Indian subcontinent that has been documented. Furthermore, our case is unique in that the ultrasound abnormalities were identified at the time of the NT scan.

Case report

A 27-year-old primigravida visited our ultrasound center at 12 weeks 6 days of gestation for an NT scan. The patient was a nonsmoker, and nonalcoholic. She was overweight with a body mass index of 28.9. There was no history of hypertension, diabetes, or thyroid disorders. There was no history of any prior abortion. Marriage was nonconsanguineous without any family history of any structural abnormalities. Her viability scan showed a single live embryo with a normal heart rate at 7 weeks.At NT scan the ultrasound showed a singleton fetus with alobar holoprosencephaly (Fig. 1, Fig. 2), subcutaneous edema over the upper body (Fig. 2) with NT measuring 1.7mm corresponding to 48th centile, hypotelorism (Fig. 3), cleft palate (Fig. 4), absent nasal bone, and single umbilical artery (SUA). Trisomy 13 was suspected based on sonography.The patient opted for termination of pregnancy. A fetal autopsy (Fig. 5) was performed which showed multiple craniofacial abnormalities including holoprosencephaly, cleft lip and cleft palate, low-set ears, largemouth, and single umbilical artery. Chromosomal microarray (Fig. 6) was also performed on the product of conception which showed ∼5.5 MB deletion in chromosome 7 spanning 7q36.2-36.3 region. Parental karyotyping done to exclude balanced translocation was normal.

Discussion

A partial deletion of the long arm of chromosome 7 causes the rare chromosomal anomaly syndrome known as terminal 7q deletion syndrome, also known as distal 7q36 monosomy. The syndrome's highly variable phenotype includes holoprosencephaly, developmental delay, growth restriction, facial abnormalities, ocular abnormalities, and other congenital malformations. Cardiopathies have also been described seldom [1].Patients with terminal 7q deletion syndrome have been documented in the literature, along with descriptions of their variable phenotypes. Nonetheless, the majority of case reports describe the diseases that are discovered after birth without a prenatal ultrasound diagnosis. Only a few cases describing the antenatal ultrasound features associated with terminal 7q deletion syndrome have been reported. Following a thorough review of the literature on PubMed, 5 examples documenting the prenatal ultrasound characteristics of this syndrome were found.Table 1 provides a summary of the cases. At 23 weeks gestation, Song et al. [3] reported a case of 7q deletion with an isolated ultrasonography finding of hemivertebra causing scoliosis in the lower thoracic spine. No other ultrasound abnormalities were seen and the patient chose to terminate the pregnancy. In a case of 21 weeks gestation, Zhao et al. [4] reported intrauterine growth retardation (IUGR), subcutaneous cystic mass in the neck, congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH), cleft lip, cleft palate, and HPE on prenatal ultrasonography. Pregnancy was terminated and genetic testing showed a loss at 7q32.3qter. Chen et al. [5] reported a case of 7q35 deletion associated with IUGR, tetralogy of Fallot (TOF), and microcephaly at 22 weeks of gestation on prenatal ultrasonography. Hallmark craniofacial abnormalities such as HPE or cleft lip/palate were absent. Prenatal ultrasound signs of HPE, proboscis, cyclopia, and IUGR were reported at 23 weeks in a case with partial trisomy 3p and monosomy 7q, according to a case report by Chen et al. [6]. At 15 weeks of gestation, Chen et al [7] reported a case of distal 3p duplication and terminal 7q deletion which was associated with nuchal edema, HPE, and cyclopia in a fetus seen on antenatal ultrasound.The antenatal ultrasound findings of this syndrome at an NT scan have only been documented in our case. This suggests that severe anomalies like HPE, cleft palate, and absent nasal bone can be detected as early as 12-13 weeks of pregnancy. Thus, our example demonstrates that anomalies related to this defect can be found on prenatal ultrasound at 12-13 weeks of gestation, allowing for prompt care and well-informed decision-making.The most frequent ultrasonography abnormality in the 6 cases, including ours, was holoprosencephaly, which was detected in 4 (67%) of the cases, followed by IUGR in 3 (50%) cases, cleft palate in 2 (33%) cases, and cyclopia (33%) in 2 cases. In our case, there was just 1 umbilical artery and no nasal bone, which have not yet been documented in the literature.It is our novel observation that terminal 7q deletion syndrome shows overlapping ultrasound features with Trisomy 13 or Patau syndrome [8]. Both can present with HPE, cyclopia, hypotelorism, cleft lip and palate, CDH, SUA, and IUGR. In our instance, hypotelorism, cleft palate, and alobar HPE were the overlapping features. To distinguish between the 2, fetal autopsy or invasive testing is required.

Conclusion

In summary, our case exhibited both novel features—such as a missing nasal bone and a single umbilical artery—and the conventional ultrasound characteristics of 7q syndrome, such as holoprosencephaly, cleft palate, and hypotelorism, thereby extending the phenotypic of 7q- syndrome prenatal ultrasonography. Our results and the literature research show that HPE and craniofacial abnormalities are the primary ultrasonography characteristics of 7q- syndrome. There may be traits that overlap with Trisomy 13, thus the differential needs to be taken into account. The abnormal findings can be picked up as early as an NT scan. For patients with abnormal prenatal ultrasound results, genetic analysis must be carried out to expeditiously guide management and decision-making.

Patient consent

A written and informed consent was obtained from the parents for publication of this case report.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 27-year-old primigravida, non-smoker, non-alcoholic, BMI: 28.9.
- **Chief Complaint**: None.
- **History of Present Illness**: None.
- **Past Medical History**: None.
- **Personal History**: No history of hypertension, diabetes, thyroid disorders, or prior abortion. Married to a non-consanguineous spouse.
- **Family History**: No history of structural abnormalities.
- **Physical Examination**: Not applicable, as this is a prenatal case.
- **Ancillary Tests**: 
  - Viability scan at 7 weeks showed a single live embryo.
  - Ultrasound at 12 weeks, 6 days gestation showed:
    - Alobar holoprosencephaly.
    - Subcutaneous edema.
    - NT: 1.7mm (48th centile).
    - Hypotelorism, cleft palate.
    - Absent nasal bone.
    - Single umbilical artery.
  - Chromosomal microarray revealed a terminal deletion of about 5.5 MB in the 7q36.2-7q36.3 region.
  - Parental karyotyping normal.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations**:
   - Ultrasound findings raised suspicion for chromosomal abnormalities, particularly Trisomy 13 (Patau Syndrome) due to overlapping features (holoprosencephaly, cleft palate, hypotelorism, single umbilical artery).
   
2. **Rationale for Suspecting Trisomy 13**:
   - Trisomy 13 frequently presents with holoprosencephaly, craniofacial abnormalities, and single umbilical artery, as observed in this case.

3. **Rationale for Excluding Trisomy 13**:
   - Diagnosis ruled out after genetic testing indicated a terminal 7q deletion encompassing 7q36.2-36.3, confirming terminal 7q deletion syndrome.

4. **Confirmation of Terminal 7q Deletion Syndrome**:
   - Ultrasound findings of holoprosencephaly, cleft palate, and craniofacial abnormalities align with reported features of 7q deletion syndrome.
   - Novel features in this case, such as absent nasal bone and single umbilical artery, expand the known phenotype of this condition.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The final diagnosis is **Terminal 7q Deletion Syndrome**. Diagnosis was confirmed via chromosomal microarray showing a terminal deletion in chromosome 7 (7q36.2-36.3). Key findings include prenatal detection of holoprosencephaly, craniofacial malformations, and a single umbilical artery.

results.content:
### Reasoning:
<step 1> **Initial Review of Patient Information and Chief Complaint:** The patient is a young, healthy primigravida with no significant medical or personal history and no chief complaint, as this is a routine prenatal assessment.  This initially suggests a low-risk pregnancy from the provided history alone.

<step 2> **Analysis of Ultrasound Findings at 12 weeks 6 days:** The ultrasound at 12 weeks 6 days reveals multiple major fetal anomalies.  Alobar holoprosencephaly is a severe brain malformation. Subcutaneous edema suggests fluid accumulation, potentially indicating hydrops fetalis or a more generalized issue. Hypotelorism, cleft palate, and absent nasal bone are facial dysmorphisms.  A single umbilical artery (SUA) is a minor anomaly but can be associated with other structural and chromosomal issues, especially in the presence of other major anomalies.  While NT is within normal limits, the constellation of other findings overshadows the normal NT value.

<step 3> **Interpreting the Chromosomal Microarray Result:** The chromosomal microarray is the most crucial piece of information. It identifies a **terminal deletion of about 5.5 MB in the 7q36.2-7q36.3 region**. This is a significant genetic abnormality. A terminal deletion means a loss of genetic material at the end of the long arm (q) of chromosome 7 in the region specified.

<step 4> **Considering Parental Karyotyping:** Normal parental karyotypes indicate that the 7q deletion is likely a *de novo* mutation, meaning it arose spontaneously in the fetus and was not inherited from either parent. This is important for recurrence risk counseling.

<step 5> **Synthesizing Ultrasound and Genetic Findings:** The severe structural anomalies observed on ultrasound, particularly alobar holoprosencephaly, are highly consistent with genetic syndromes and chromosomal abnormalities. The definitive finding of a 7q36.2-7q36.3 terminal deletion strongly links the observed phenotype to this specific genetic defect.  The combination of holoprosencephaly, facial dysmorphisms (hypotelorism, cleft palate, absent nasal bone), and subcutaneous edema, along with the SUA, is a classic presentation for certain chromosomal deletion syndromes, and in this case, is specifically explained by the identified 7q terminal deletion.

<step 6> **Formulating the Final Diagnosis:** Based on the definitive chromosomal microarray result and the concordant ultrasound findings, the final diagnosis is a genetic condition resulting from the identified chromosomal deletion.  The most accurate and informative diagnosis is to state the specific chromosomal abnormality detected by the microarray, and acknowledge that the observed phenotype is consistent with this genetic defect. While a specific syndrome name might not be explicitly given based on the provided information alone, the diagnosis should clearly indicate the chromosomal basis of the fetal anomalies.

### Answer:
Fetal diagnosis of 7q36.2-7q36.3 terminal deletion, consistent with ultrasound findings of alobar holoprosencephaly, subcutaneous edema, hypotelorism, cleft palate, absent nasal bone, and single umbilical artery.
